paper_title: Robusto-2: Benchmarking Humans & VLMs for Autonomous Driving in Lima & New York City

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As Self-Driving Cars continue to expand internationally and use multi-modal systems such as VLMs as a cognitive backbone for their Action models; how well will these systems generalize in new settings, in particular out-of-distribution (OOD) edge-case scenarios in new geographies? In this paper, we study this open question by providing a full factorial analysis with human drivers of Lima, human drivers from New York City, and VLMs and showing them dashcam footage collected from Lima and New York City -- prompting them with a variety of questions under a Visual Question Answering (VQA) paradigm. In particular, we pick these two cities as they are highly challenging driving locations where no Self-Driving Car company currently operates in, and ask questions that span 4 categories: Factual, Ratings, Counterfactual and Reasoning. We find that Humans and VLMs diverge in their responses -- though this is modulated by the type of questions asked, and that Humans answer similarly independent of where they are from (Lima/NYC). To our surprise, we did not find a strong difference in terms of answers (Humans or VLMs) that was modulated by geography, likely due to their high out-of-distribution nature. Our dataset is available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/Artificio/robusto-2

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 8 1 ] . [ 1 0 8 9 0 2 . 6 0 6 2 : 1 How well do VLMs cross generalize to new driving scenarios? Today, the algorithms powering self-driving car technology have slowly been evolving ranging from hard-coded heuristics [14, 35, 42], to end-to-end models [10, 12] and now reversing to middle-ground of end-to-end and yet modular systems such as Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models powered by Vision-Language models (VLMs) as cognitive backbones [38, 39, 51]. Despite this exciting and fast-paced evolution in academia, very little research has been focusing on evaluating how such models perform within the real world, and more specifically to different geographies and chaotic environments [15, 17, 44, 50]. 2 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Fig. 1: How well do Humans from Lima, Humans from NYC and VLMs compare to each other cognitively when being shown Out-of-Distribution video clips (A.)? In this paper we study the cross-cultural (Lima vs NYC) and cross-system (Human vs VLM) generalization gap when prompted with data from both geographies. Studying this gap under VQA framework (B.) is important for explainability purposes and as self-driving cars continue to expand outside pioneering cities like San Francisco and Shenzhen. In particular we will aim to disentangle interactions between the individuals and video data from both Lima & NYC (C.). In this paper we are motivated to answer the previous question, but also within the context of how markets evolve in the deployment and adaptation of self-driving car technology. As of today, New York City (USA) and Lima (Peru), remain as two of the most challenging cities to drive in globally [9, 32], and are both cities that do not have self-driving cars deployed likely both due to their complexity and regulation (unlike San Francisco, London, Dubai or Shenzhen where several startups have already started deploying their fleets). In that regard, it is important to perform studies assessing the cross-cultural generalization capacity of these systems because most multi-modal foundation models [15,29,36] be it VLMs or VLAs are trained with Western Hemisphere data, mainly from the United States [33, 41]. This raises an important safety question: when these systems are later deployed in other parts of the world [20] in particular to locations where little to no data is collected for training or validation, how well will they generalize? And how big is this cross-cultural generalization gap, if it exists? To answer this question we collect series of dashcam driving videos recorded from the same camera from both Lima and New York City; and perform Visual Questions Answering [3] on collection of 3 populations: humans from Lima, humans from New York City and VLMs from different start-ups & organizations as shown in Figure 1. This allows us to create fully factorial design of 2 Robusto-2 3 3 where we can study how answers compare across humans of these 2 cities and VLMs contingent on where the data was recorded from, and to whom the questions were asked. Our Human and VLM data in addition to the stimuli & analysis will be open-sourced.

sections.title: 2 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Previous work such as Robusto-1 [15] has shown that Humans and VLMs either converged or diverged in their answers contingent on the types of questions asked (Factual, Multiple-Choice or Counter-Factual & Hypotheticals) when prompted O.O.D. video clips from Lima, Peru. Furthermore, there has been more recent work that has benchmarked several VLMs models within the context of Autonomous Driving and also using Visual Question Answering (VQA) [3], as it allows us to cognitively probe each system given their answer [5, 27, 31]. In this paper we will extend the analysis by adding control group from New York City: both subjects and video data; as one could perhaps imagine that it is unfair to compare humans from Lima (a non-US city with non-native english speakers) to VLMs trained in US-based data as was done in Robusto-1 [15]. This nuance is important because questions in the previously mentioned study were asked in english. We provide details of our experimental setup in the rest of this section.

sections.title: 2.1 Participants
sections.content: A total of 10 humans from Lima, 10 humans from New York City and 10 VLMs were used (totalling 30 systems). All questions were asked in english to all systems for the Visual Question Answering (VQA) procedure. Human participants were recruited through an open call published on our organizations official LinkedIn account and digital online ads targeting active university students aged between 18 and 30 years. Interested candidates completed pre-registration screening survey. To proceed, candidates were required to verify their student status and hold valid drivers license for their respective cities. Eligible candidates then entered verification and trial phase. They submitted their official documentation and completed pilot trial consisting of VQA tasks for three videos from Robusto-1 [15]. This trial served to familiarize participants with the task dynamics and verify the English proficiency of non-native speakers (specifically from Lima). Candidates who failed to meet the verification criteria or did not complete the trial were disqualified, and replacements were selected from the pre-registration pool. In conditions where the candidate pool exceeded the required sample size, random lottery was utilized for selection. All data collection was hosted via Google Forms. To bypass global character response limits imposed by the platform, the evaluation was split into two sequential forms containing 10 videos each, maintaining the exact question structure and unique presentation order. Access to the forms was restricted to verified emails provided during pre-registration. 4 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Fig. 2: diagram explaining the OOD selection pipeline: 200 video clips from Lima and NYC are fed to multiple VLMs by asking them the same 20 questions via VQA. We then select the 10 clips of Lima and 10 clips of NYC that have the highest variance in answers across models, interpreting this disagreement as OOD scenes. These clips are then shown to Humans in our survey. Specific logistical challenges, variable recruitment timelines, detailed compensation structures (including geographic adjustments and referral bonuses), and specific drivers license requirements are further detailed in the Supplement.

sections.title: 2.2 Clips extraction
sections.content: The raw video recordings were processed by extracting the most relevant segments, resulting in total of 200 5-second clips (half recorded in New York City and the other half in Lima). Each clip was then annotated with metadata describing its key characteristics, including the ego-vehicles actions, the behavior of surrounding vehicles and pedestrians, the state of traffic lights, as well as sky and weather conditions. From these annotated clips, the first block of videospecific questions was automatically generated, while the remaining three blocks were predefined. Based on the VQA responses, embeddings were computed and used to perform variance-based analysis across videos. For each video, the answers were divided into four blocks, and the standard deviation along each embedding dimension was calculated and ranked in descending order. This procedure enabled the identification of out-of-distribution (OOD) samples by highlighting videos with higher dispersion across their embedding space. To ensure consistency, potential overlaps between videos were removed, and the ranked clips were manually inspected to confirm their semantic validity. The resulting OOD videos were then selected to poll humans from NYC and humans from Lima. This pipeline is summarized in Figure 2 and Figure 1 (B.).

sections.title: 2.3 Questions for Visual Question Answering (VQA)
sections.content: In the following sub-section we will provide details of the type of questions asked in the Visual Question Answering experiment for Humans and VLMs. full list of the 20 questions can be seen in the Supplementary Material. Robusto-2 5 Block 1: Factual (Q1 Q5) These questions were factual questions that were related to weather, time of day, traffic signals and flow. For example, for one video; two different samples of 5 questions may look like: Examples of these questions asked in the survey were: Q1: What is the ego vehicles current action? Q2: Why is the vehicle maintaining straight path? While for another video the first five questions may look like: Q1: What action is the ego vehicle taking? Q2: Why is the vehicle braking? It is important to note that these questions varied per each video and were consistently different. In other words, every video had 5 unique factual questions, but every participants (Human or VLM) saw the same set of questions per video. Block 2: Ratings (Q6 Q10) These are questions that we ask across all systems to test how they quantitatively assess several quantities such as clutter, hazard and probabilistic outcomes more generally to see how they make perceptual and cognitive judgments. These questions are fixed for all videos, two of the five can be seen below: Q6 : Please rate the level of clutter from 1 to 10. Consider 10 as the highest level of clutter and 1 as the lowest. Q7 : On scale from 1 (not likely) to 10 (very likely), how likely is it that you encounter similar driving scenario in your regular driving experience? Block 3: CounterFactual & Hypothetical (Q11 Q15) These questions are generated to explore the creativity of both Humans and VLMs, as these questions objectively do not have proper ground truth. Here are 2 of the 5 questions we systematically ask across all systems. Q11 : What would have had to happen in this video for crash to have occured involving the driver? Q12 : What would have had to happen in this video for an external crash to have occurred not involving the driver? Block 4: Reasoning (Q16 Q20) This block is an extension that was not included in Robusto-1 [15], as models now include long Chain-of-Thought primitives. To identify how similar all systems reason (Humans vs VLMs), we ask them these reasoning questions once again many of these questions are ill-posed and difficult as it has been suggested that VLMs still struggle [11, 26]. Two of these five questions can be read below: Q16 : Which vehicle has the right of way at this intersection, based on the traffic rules and the current situation? Q17 : Is it currently safe for pedestrians to cross the street? Please explain your reasoning. Cespedes, Chincha et al. Fig. 3: An assessment of how Humans and VLMs converge/diverge depending on the questions asked and the geography of both the data and the participant for Lima and NYC locations. In (A.) we see the raw answers projected in 2D via PCA in block-wise fashion to directly compare Lima vs NYC per block, in (B.) we see the Representational Similarity Matrix (RSA) across all systems Humans & VLMs are shown in (C.).

sections.title: Machines across Lima and New York City
sections.content: The first analysis we performed revolved around knowing the differences between humans from Lima, humans from NYC, and VLMs. To accomplish this we projected all the answers through sentence embeddings for each system to 2D plot via PCA and also performed Representational Similarity Analysis [24, 25] on such embeddings to intuitively show the similarities and differences across all systems per block of questions (Figure 3). Generally, we observe little to no difference of pattern of results whether the data is from Lima or NYC, but it is rather modulated by the type of questions asked across Humans and VLMs (eg. Block 1 vs Block 2). We believe the differences across geographies are subtle because the questions asked steer the space of answers in narrower subspace. The PCA plot shown in Figure 3 (A.) high-lights these similarities as block-wise plots of the raw embeddings. In addition we also ran global PCA analysis and these projections can be seen in the Supplementary Material. Performing both of these analysis is relevant Robusto-2 7 Fig. 4: An averaged per Geography (Lima vs NYC) Multi-Dimensional Scaling plot computed via the L1 distances of ratings across all questions of Block 2 (Q6 - Q10) showing all Humans and VLMs. Notice that the geometry of ratings is very similar across Geographies, and that Humans are very similar to each other, but not so much to VLMs however, this Human vs VLM difference is accentuated in Lima. to inspect the local block-wise and global differences of the answer even if the projections are linear. We also replicated this analysis with another embedding model (Qwen3-Embedding-4B [47]) instead of all-mpnet-base-v2 [40] and see how the general pattern of results hold as observed in the Supplement.

sections.title: 4 Analysis of Systematic Bias
sections.content: Our next analysis consisted of studying how Humans and VLMs responded to the set of 5 questions we have that involved 1 to 10 rating scale. Recall from Block 2, that these questions are subjective by nature, and were asked across all videos to each VLM and Human as follows: Q6: Please rate the level of clutter from 1 to 10. Consider 10 as the highest level of clutter and 1 as the lowest. Q7: On scale from 1 (not likely) to 10 (very likely), how likely is it that you encounter similar driving scenario in your regular driving experience? Q8: On scale from 1 (not hazardous) to 10 (very hazardous), how hazardous is the situation for the driver? Q9: On scale from 1 to 10, how well do you think an autonomous vehicle would handle driving in this scene? Consider 1 as very poor driving and 10 as perfect driving. Q10: On scale from 1 to 10, how well do you think you could handle driving in this scene? Consider 1 as very poor driving and 10 as perfect driving. The first analysis we did was to perform Multi-Dimensional Scaling procedure on the dissimilarity matrix computed by the raw numeric scores across all systems per video. This difference is computed using the L1 distance of each 8 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Fig. 5: Analysis of Systematic bias covered through distributions (A.) and inter-system differences per video (B). In (A.) we compute the average ratings of each system via 1 repetition, and compare these to violin plots computer for each VLM per 30-fold repetitions per video. In (B.) we compute the L1 distance between all pairs of each systems ratings, and compute an average across all videos per question. In both plots, there is high similarity across systems when shown videos from either Lima or NYC. rating and done in fully factorial manner across all systems pairs. The per Geography average of such results is shown in Figure 4 where we observe that Humans cluster together independently of where the data is from (Lima or NYC). We do find that VLMs however seem to be further away from the Human cluster in Lima than in New York City, and that Humans from New York City seem to have less of difference with VLMs than Humans from Lima in both geographies. We then wanted to analyze the pattern of ratings as distributions of each system (Humans and VLMs), and in particular for VLMs that when prompted multiple times may provide different ratings. To produce distribution, we made each VLM respond to every video-question pair 20 times, we used these repeated measurements to construct compound violin plots capturing the distribution of ratings. The responses were first separated by video region (Lima on the top row and NYC on the bottom row). For each of the five questions, we generated Robusto-2 9 dedicated subplot. In each subplot, every violin represents the full distribution of ratings (including the 20 repetitions) produced by specific VLM for that question within given region (e.g., all ratings from Cosmos Reason for Question 6 across Lima videos). This result can be seen in Figure 5 (A.). Additionally, each subplot includes two human consensus references, one for Lima (red) and one for NYC (blue), computed as the average rating across participants for each corresponding human region, video region, and question combination. We also added VLM consensus (green) by averaging over the 1st repetition of the 20 used to produce the distribution across videos to control for the number of points used to compute such distribution. We then calculated the Wasserstein distance averaged across all violin plots averaged over the VLMs. These are reported in the lower row of the plot. We noticeably found that the most similar pattern of ratings across VLMs was for Question 8: On scale from 1 (non hazardous) to 10 (very hazardous), how hazardous is the situation for the driver? signaling that systems respond quite similarly independent of the geography (videos from Lima or videos NYC). Meanwhile Question 9: On scale from 1 to 10, how well do you think an autonomous vehicle would handle driving in this scene? Consider 1 as very poor driving and 10 as perfect driving got the highest distance suggesting an effect of the geography when VLMs are asked to answer such question pertaining self-awareness of driving patterns. As reference to the first systematic bias analysis that was based on differences rather than individual ratings, we contrasted them both in Figure 5 (A.) and (B.) at the per question per geography level. What is most noticeable is that there is little column-wise variation suggestion that the pattern of ratings are independent of geography even though there is some mild tendency for larger differences in Lima vs NYC.

sections.title: 5 Semantic Similarity via LLM-as-a-Judge
sections.content: To further evaluate the semantic alignment between the responses provided by humans and VLMs, we implemented an LLM-as-a-judge [49] framework using Qwen3-4B model in thinking mode as the judger. This experiment followed two-stage pipeline designed to perform pairwise comparisons between all participating systems (VLMs, Lima participants, and NYC participants) across all videos and questions. The evaluation was conducted by presenting the judge with specific question and two candidate answers derived from the same video-question pair. In the first stage, binary prompt was used to determine semantic comparability; specifically, whether the two answers could be meaningfully contrasted. Those approved pairs were used in the second stage, in which the judge was provided with scoring rubric to assess the degree of alignment between the pairs. This rubric utilized scale of four values: 2 (strong agreement), 1 (partial agreement), 1 (partial disagreement), and 2 (strong disagreement). Pairs assigned score of 0 in the first stage, considered not meaningfully comparable, were excluded 10 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Fig. 6: Semantic Similarity via LLM-as-a-Judge. Here we observe how different the answers were per systems across the 3 blocks: Factual, Counterfactual and Reasoning. Given that LLM-as-a-Judge has better semantic understanding than embeddings that are driven by syntaxis, differences per block begin to emerge. Notice however that the pattern of answers for Humans and VLMs is very similar for videos from both Lima and NYC. from this stage and did not receive rubric score. Of the total pairs evaluated, 63,121 passed to the second stage in Block 1, 57,886 in Block 3, and 61,302 in Block 4, representing passage rates of 86.6%, 79.4%, and 84.1% respectively. It should be noted that Block 2 was excluded from this analysis. Because Block 2 required the assessment of purely numeric values, the judge exhibited significant inconsistency between runs, often yielding different scores for identical pairs of numeric ratings. Details like the prompt used for each stage can be found in the Supplement. Figure 6 presents the results of this stage as series of heatmaps. These matrices visualize the average agreement scores between every pair of systems, separated by video region (Lima and NYC) and question block (Blocks 1, 3, and 4). The color scale represents the mean agreement score, ranging from strong disagreement (pink) to strong agreement (violet), allowing for direct comparison of how closely all systems (Humans and VLMs) converge/diverge across different geographical contexts.

sections.title: 6 Discussion
sections.content: As Autonomous Vehicles start being deployed globally, studying the cross-cultural generalization gap from the perspective of security, robustness and explainability, Robusto-2 11 is important because VLMs are being used as VLA backbones (eg. AlpamayoR1 [43] and OpenVLA [23]) that requires model calibration [19, 28, 34]. In this paper we have focused on the cognitive assessment of systems: humans and VLMs (the cognitive back-bone of VLAs) with focus on data from NYC and Lima, with the extra control of also having 2 sub-groups of humans: those who live in NYC and also in Lima. We have found that: 1) large differences in cognitive alignment were not found when showing data from Lima vs NYC for both Humans and VLMs; 2) humans from Lima and NYC generally share very similar answering patterns independent of the data they were shown; 3) Humans and VLMs converge/diverge in answers depending on the nature of questions asked. 4) VLM models share very similar answering patterns independent of where they were trained from and what training procedure they underwent (see Platonic Representation hypothesis [7, 8, 16, 21, 30]); Although these results are very interesting, it is important to highlight that one of our limitations is also key strength. Specifically, we performed this study with only 20 5-second clips and asked 20 questions to each system (humans from Lima, Human for NYC or VLM; total of 30 subjects); this totals 400 data points per subject, i.e. 12000 data points. Generally speaking, it is very low number of data points but while this on the surface may seem low, we emphasize that we have the same number of data points and observations to evaluate interobserver differences across systems, that in many other studies is not possible due to data imbalance where human data relies on aggregate answers from humans on Amazon Mechanical Turk or other crowd-sourcing platforms that pool human data into single Mega-Human observer. In this paper, we have successfully been able to dissect inter-system differences and similarities across all humans from Lima and New York City and VLMs, and plan to expand next version of the paper to include larger populations and video data from 5 continents. Indeed, the main bottleneck we have found is gathering the human data rather than computing the VLM models. Finally, let us revisit the question posed at the start of this paper: Is there cross-cultural generalization gap between humans and VLMs between Lima and New York City? Yes and no. There is gap when comparing both humans to VLMs for both cities, however there is not gap when observing the general pattern of responses of systems (Humans and VLMs) between geographies, suggesting they are both chaotic in similar ways. Ultimately, as most OOD data analysis in Self-Driving is done in simulation [1, 18, 22], we have found an interesting advantage for the collection of dashcam/driving data from different cities in the world that while chaotic, may be useful to train/test future autonomous vehicle models for both research and production. 12 Cespedes, Chincha et al.

sections.title: 6.1 Participants
sections.content: Humans of Lima: total of 10 human subjects between the ages of 18 to 30 with valid drivers license were openly called from Lima, Peru through digital online add across several universities. It was pre-requisite for all human subjects to have valid Peruvian drivers license at the time of the survey, in addition to being full-time student (undergraduate and/or post-graduate). Fluency in english was also required by subjects from Peru where spanish is generally their native language. All answers were recorded in english, and questions were also asked in english. Participants were paid $50 at the time of survey completion. It took roughly 1 month for us to find 10 participants. Humans of New York City: To keep recruiting conditions the same, we did an open-call similar to students in Lima for students in New York City to complete our study. Recruiting subjects for New York City was more challenging because most students do not drive if they live in New York City, and usually take the Metro. Furthermore it was difficult to find subjects who would be willing to do the study for $50 given the international wage gap and US dollar to Peruvian Sol conversion (3.3 soles 1 US Dollar) . The first 2 subjects were paid $50, while the rest were paid $100 to increase the monetary incentive after several user feedback about the payment of participants. In some cases $25 referral bonuses were made to subjects who after completing the survey managed to recruit friends willing to do the experiment. Subjects were required to be fluent in english, and to have valid drivers license in some cases mixes of local US drivers license and valid international/ US inter-state drivers licenses were valid as some exchange students signed up for the study. In other words, as long as they had document that could let them legally drive in New York City, they were eligible for the study. Naturally, given the cosmopolitan nature of New York City, not all subjects were American Citizens, however all subjects had to be NYC-university affiliated students living in New York City at the time. It took roughly 6 months for us to find 10 participants that could complete the study. It took about 6 hours for subjects in Lima or NYC to complete the study. Vision Language Models (VLMs): total of 10 VLMs were used to answer the same questions asked to the Human subjects of both Lima and NYC. Questions were asked in English, and the models that were used sampled wide range of companies from different locations such as: United States of America and China. The models that we ran were: Cosmos Reason 8B, Gemini 3 Flash Preview, Gemini 3 Pro Preview, InternVL3 8B, LLaVA-Video-7B-Qwen2, MiniCPM-o 2.6, PerceptionLM 8B - Phi4 Multimodal Instruct, Qwen3-VL 8B Instruct, VideoLLama3 7B. sections.title: 6.3 Data Acquisition and Clip Extraction
sections.content: The raw footage used in this study consists of high-definition dashcam (BlackVue DR970X Plus) recordings (1080p at 30 fps) captured in diverse urban environments across New York City (USA) and Lima (Peru). To ensure high density of complex driving scenarios, we developed custom GUI-based extraction tool using Python and the Qt framework. This tool enabled us to parse several hours of raw footage and extract 5-second segments containing out-of-distribution (OOD) or high-complexity events. The selection criteria focused on: Anomalous agent behavior: Drivers cutting in, sudden braking, or illegal maneuvers. Vulnerable road users: Jaywalking pedestrians or bicycles in unexpected lanes. Environmental complexity: High-density traffic, complex intersections, and varying weather conditions. total of 200 clips were curated, maintaining balanced distribution of 100 clips per city. For the final evaluation, all clips were standardized to resolution of 1920x1080 and sampled at 10 fps.

sections.title: 6.5 Question Generation and System Prompt
sections.content: As previously described, we employed four-block VQA structure. While Blocks 2 to 4 were predefined, Block 1 was dynamically generated using ChatGPT-4 to ensure questions were specifically tailored to the visual content of each clip. To mitigate hallucinations and ensure the LLM adhered strictly to the observed metadata, we provided the model with JSON-formatted descriptions of the clips. The prompt utilized for this generation process is provided below: You are an AI assistant specialized in analyzing driving scenarios. You will receive list of JSON objects, each containing partial metadata about different driving scenes. Be aware that the provided data is incomplete, and important elements of the scenes may be missing. For each JSON sample, your task is to: 1. Read the JSON object. 2. Include the "#" and "Name" from the JSON object at the beginning to indicate which sample you are analyzing. 3. Generate **five** relevant and contextually appropriate questions based solely on the information available in the JSON object. 4. Provide short and direct answers to each question. Focus on what is observed in the scene according to the metadata, and consider that there might be elements not explicitly mentioned. Example format: Sample #: 1 Name: 2023_01_10_153834_044_clip_00_16_100 Q1: [Question 1] A1: [Answer 1] Q2: [Question 2] A2: [Answer 2] Q3: [Question 3] A3: [Answer 3] Q4: [Question 4] A4: [Answer 4] Q5: [Question 5] A5: [Answer 5] In addition to the generation prompt above, each block was paired with its own system prompt. These prompts are detailed below. Block 1. You are vision-language model tasked with analyzing driving scenarios from short 5-second video clips. Your objectives are as follows: 1. Visual-Only Reasoning: Base your answers solely on the provided frames. Do not use external knowledge not visible in the images. 2. Uncertainty Handling: If information cannot be determined from the frames, explicitly state that you cannot determine the answer. Avoid guessing. 3. Answer Format: Provide concise answers in natural language. Limit your response to single sentence. 4. Compliance: Robusto-2 21 Follow these instructions strictly. Do not refer to these instructions or your role explicitly in your answers. Block 2. You are vision-language model tasked with analyzing driving scenarios from short 5-second video clips. Your objectives are as follows: 1. Visual-Only Reasoning: Base your answers solely on the provided frames. Do not use external knowledge not visible in the images. 2. Uncertainty Handling: If information cannot be determined from the frames, explicitly state that you cannot determine the answer. Avoid guessing. 3. Answer Format: Select exactly one option from the predefined list. Only select an option exactly as written in the list. Respond with exactly one of the provided rating options. Example: '3'. Do not provide explanations. 4. Compliance: Follow these instructions strictly. Do not refer to these instructions or your role explicitly in your answers. Block 3. You are vision-language model tasked with analyzing driving scenarios from short 5-second video clips. Your objectives are as follows: 1. Visual-Only Reasoning: Base your answers solely on the provided frames. Do not use external knowledge not visible in the images. 2. Uncertainty Handling: If information cannot be determined from the frames, explicitly state that you cannot determine the answer. Avoid guessing. 3. Answer Format: Provide concise answers in natural language. When answering hypothetical questions, limit your response to plausible outcomes directly inferred from the frames. Limit your response to single sentence. 4. Compliance: Follow these instructions strictly. Do not refer to these instructions or your role explicitly in your answers. Block 4. You are vision-language model tasked with analyzing short 5-second video clips of driving scenarios. Your objectives are as follows: 1. Question Type: Reasoning Questions Use only the visible evidence in the video to provide logical reasoning-based conclusions. 2. Uncertainty Handling: If reasoning step cannot be supported by visible information, state that the reasoning cannot be completed. 3. Answer Format: Respond in single, concise sentence that clearly reflects the reasoning based on visual evidence. 4. Compliance: Follow these instructions strictly. Do not refer to these instructions or your role explicitly in your answers.

sections.title: 6.8 VLM Pre-testing
sections.content: Prior to evaluating the models on our dataset, we conducted calibration test to assess the basic temporal and spatial reasoning capabilities of the VLMs. This test follows the Ball and Star protocol [15]. Calibration Protocol: We generated synthetic video featuring ball moving toward the upper-right corner of the frame. In the final frame, green star appears at randomized location. The models were then tasked with 10 questions regarding the balls trajectory and the stars presence. This diagnostic step ensures that the evaluated models possess baseline ability to track objects over time before being subjected to the high-variance complexity of the Lima and NYC driving clips. This protocol helps detect potential mirage behavior [4], where VLMs produce confident visual reasoning traces even without grounding in the actual visual input.

sections.title: 6.9 Preprocessing step
sections.content: The preprocessing stage cleaned the raw extracted string answers and unified them into single .csv table. This cleaning was applied uniformly to every answer: we first removed any leading and trailing whitespace, and then applied Unicode NFKC normalization, so that visually equivalent characters were mapped to single canonical form and the text remained consistent for the downstream embedding step. After the previous cleaning, Block 2 required one additional step. Since its questions expect numerical rating but some VLMs answered with free-text rather than single number, we introduced an extraction phase that recovers valid integer in the range [1, 10] from each answer, using the following procedure: The rating is extracted from each answer by applying the first matching rule: 1. If is not string, return nan. 2. If the stripped string parses to an integer [1, 10], return n. 3. If it matches out of with [1, 10] and x, return x. 4. Otherwise, among all standalone numbers in [1, 10], return the last one. 5. If none apply, return nan. Finally, we retained two versions of the dataset: one without this extraction phase, kept for logging and traceability, and the fully processed one, in which the cleaning heuristic was applied. The latter is the version used in all subsequent Block 2 analyses. After this stage, and consistently across all subsequent analyses and metrics, Block 2 answers were treated as integers in [1, 10], whereas those from the remaining blocks were kept as free-text strings. Cespedes, Chincha et al.

sections.content: RSA [25] sidesteps the paraphrase issue by comparing the geometry each system imposes on the stimulus set, rather than the embeddings themselves. The intuition: two systems agree if, when they look at the same set of items, they find the same items similar to each other. Step 1 per-system fingerprint. sections.title: 6.15 Systematic-Bias MDS (Block 2)
sections.content: The bias stage above (Sec. 6.14) compares each system to human consensus per question. Here we instead ask how far apart any pair of systems sits on the Likert scale, and embed those pairwise gaps as 2D map so the global structure of rating disagreement is visible at glance. As in Sec. 6.14 we use only Block 2 (the 110 numeric questions Q6Q10), repetition = 1, and the numeric rating xa,v,q obtained by parsing the textual answer (unparseable answers become NaN). Robusto-2 33 Step 1 pairwise magnitude-difference matrix. For fixed question and video region r, the dissimilarity between two systems a, is the mean absolute gap between their ratings over the videos of that region, (q,r) a,a = mean v: region(v)=r xa,v,q, xa,v,q defined (cid:12) (cid:12) xa,v,q xa,v,q (cid:12) (cid:12). (10) The average uses nanmean: video on which either system answered NaN is skipped for that pair only, so missing answers do not void the whole cell and no values are fabricated; pair with no shared video stays NaN. (q,r) is symmetric with zero diagonal. We also form question-pooled version (r) that averages (cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12)xa,v,q xa,v,q (cid:12) over all Block-2 questions of region at once. These matrices are shown directly as 25 grid of heatmaps (regions questions) on shared color scale, so cities and questions can be compared against one another. Step 2 embed systems as points (MDS). We treat each as dissimilarity matrix δ and recover 2D configuration = (xa)a, one point per system, whose Euclidean distances reproduce δ. We use classical (Torgerson) MDS (sklearn.manifold.ClassicalMDS, n_components=2, metric="precomputed"), which double-centres δ and takes the top two eigenvectors of the resulting Gram matrix. Goodness of fit is reported as Kruskals normalized stress-1 between the input dissimilarities and the embedded distances, (cid:118) (cid:117) (cid:117) (cid:116) σ = (cid:80) (cid:0)xi xj δij i<j (cid:80) i<jxi xj2 (cid:1)2 , (11) where pairs with δij = NaN are dropped from both sums; lower is better and σ < 0.1 indicates good fit. The configuration is only defined up to rotation and reflection, so absolute axis orientation may differ between panels and only relative point positions carry meaning. Color encodes the system family and each system is drawn as its provider icon (humans fall back to per-group marker), matching the legend convention of the PCA plot (Sec. 6.11). We show one MDS panel per region for the question-pooled (r), and 25 grid for the per-question (q,r).

sections.title: 6.16 LLM-as-a-Judge Agreement
sections.content: Embedding similarity (Sec. 6.12) cannot distinguish and answer different questions from and answer the same question with opposite claims: both produce low cosine similarity. We close this gap by replacing the embedding proxy with prompt-grounded LLM judge that performs the same task in two sequential decisions. We use Qwen3-4B with chain-of-thought enabled, sampling parameters =1.0, top-p 0.95, top-k 20, presence penalty 1.5. Block 2 (numeric ratings) is excluded the bias stage already covers it. 34 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Stage 1 comparability. Given the question and the two answers, judge J1 outputs s(1) v,q,a,a = J1 (cid:0)q, ya,v,q, ya,v,q (cid:1) {0, 1}. (12) s(1)=1 means the two answers are about the same thing and can be checked for agreement; s(1)=0 means they talk past each other (different referent, abstention, etc.) and there is nothing to score. The system prompt used is the following: You are an expert logical analyst. Task: Determine if Response and Response provide claims that can be compared for agreement or contradiction regarding the [Question]. Rules: - Mark as 1 (COMPARABLE) if: 1. Both responses provide factual answer to the [Question]. 2. They describe the same object's state (e.g., if the question asks "What is the car doing?", any description of its motion-turning, stopping, or going straight-is COMPARABLE because these are competing descriptions of the same event). 3. The responses are "on the same page" even if they disagree (e.g., "Yes" vs "No"). - Mark as 0 (NOT_COMPARABLE) if: 1. The responses "talk past each other" (e.g., Question: "What is the car doing?"; A: "It's turning"; B: "It's blue car"). One describes an action, the other describes an appearance. These are NOT comparable. 2. One response provides facts while the other says "I don't know," "I can't see," or is empty. 3. They discuss different objects entirely. [Few-Shot Examples] Question: "What is the ego vehicle's action?" A: "Turning right." B: "Moving forward." -> 1 (Comparable: These are two different descriptions of the vehicle's trajectory. If one is true, the other is likely false.) A: "Braking." B: "Stopped." -> 1 (Comparable: These both describe the vehicle's speed/state.) A: "Accelerating." B: "The car is black." -> 0 (Not Comparable: describes motion, describes color. They do not overlap or conflict.) Question: "Is there traffic light?" A: "Yes, it is green." B: "No." -> 1 (Comparable: One confirms existence, the other denies it.) Output ONLY valid JSON: { } "Evaluation": "Briefly explain your reasoning.", "Score": 1 0 Stage 2 signed agreement. For pairs that passed Stage 1, second judge J2 scores agreement on four-point scale: s(2) v,q,a,a = (cid:0)q, ya,v,q, ya,v,q (cid:1) {2, 1, +1, +2}, (13) with the semantics in Table 4. The system prompt used is the following: You are strict and impartial evaluator of factual alignment. Task: Compare the semantic agreement between Response and Response regarding the [Question]. Robusto-2 35 s(2) Meaning +2 strong agreement (same core conclusion, any extra specificity is fine) +1 agree on the core, one answer adds an unrelated side-fact 1 agree on the object/action, disagree on degree or intensity 2 direct contradiction Table 4: Stage-2 agreement scale used by J2. Rules: 1. **Conclusion Priority:** If both responses reach the same core conclusion (e.g., both say "Yes," both say "Safe," or both identify the same action like "Braking"), you MUST score +2. 2. **The "Zoom" Rule (Specificity):** Do not penalize for detail. "A vehicle" and "A red Toyota" are perfect match (+2) because they describe the same entity without contradiction. 3. **The "Bonus Fact" Rule (+1):** Use +1 ONLY if the responses agree on the core answer, but one response includes an *additional, separate factual claim* that the other does not mention (e.g., A: "The light is red"; B: "The light is red and there is pedestrian"). 4. **Contradictions:** Use negative scores if the responses make claims that cannot both be true. Scoring Scale: +2 (Strong Agreement): Same core conclusion. This includes cases where one is simply more specific/descriptive than the other (e.g., "moving" vs "accelerating"). +1 (Partial Agreement): Agreement on the core fact, but one response mentions an additional, unrelated detail about the scene that the other omits. -1 (Partial Contradiction): Agreement on the object/action, but disagreement on the *degree* or *intensity* (e.g., "moving fast" vs "moving slowly"). -2 (Direct Contradiction): Logically opposite claims (e.g., "Turning" vs "Straight", "Red" vs "Green", "Yes" vs "No"). [Few-Shot Examples] Question: "What is the ego vehicle doing?" A: "It is moving." B: "The vehicle is accelerating forward." -> Score: 2 (Reason: Both agree on the core action of motion. is just more specific). Question: "Is there car in front?" A: "Yes." B: "Yes, and it is blue truck." -> Score: 2 (Reason: The core conclusion to the question is identical). Question: "What is the traffic light color?" A: "Red." B: "Red. Also, the road is wet." -> Score: 1 (Reason: They agree on the light, but adds separate fact about the weather/road). Question: "How is the car moving?" A: "Moving fast." B: "Moving slowly." -> Score: -1 (Reason: They agree it is moving, but contradict on the degree of speed). Question: "What is the ego vehicle's action?" A: "Turning right." B: "Moving forward in the middle lane." -> Score: -2 (Reason: These are mutually exclusive trajectories). Output ONLY valid JSON: { "Evaluation": "Briefly explain your reasoning.", "Score": 2 1 -1 -2 } Per-pair score and aggregation. Non-comparable pairs are excluded from the agreement matrix (they are reported separately as the comparability rate, be36 Cespedes, Chincha et al. low). The final score for single item is Sv,q,a,a = (cid:40) s(2) v,q,a,a NaN s(1) v,q,a,a = 1, s(1) v,q,a,a = 0, (14) where Nan is considered non-comparable pair. Then the heatmap entry for block b, region is the mean over the comparable items of that cell, (b,r) a,a = mean (v,q) block b, region s(1) v,q,a,a =1 s(2) v,q,a,a [2, +2]. (15) Comparability rate. We also report the fraction of pairs that even reached Stage 2, π(b,r) = mean (v,q,a,a) (b,r) s(1) v,q,a,a [0, 1], (16) so the reader can separate these systems disagree (low J, high π) from these systems are not even answering the same question (any J, low π). Reliability. Pairs are deduplicated by sorting (a, a), so each unordered pair is scored once and the heatmap is symmetric. JSON-parse failures are retried with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts; rows that still fail are kept in pending bucket separate from non-comparable, and are not counted in either (b,r) or π(b,r). Further follow-on questions and answers in the sample confirm that NVIDIA Cosmos can not cognitively understand what is really going on in the video. 40 Cespedes, Chincha et al. Here Qwen successfully manages some degree of alignment with the humans from Lima and NYC, although it does not fully seem to understand the subtleness of pedestrian who is jay walking under the influence.
